Hemodynamic Disorders, Thromboembolic Diseases & Shock

Pathology Lab LE 1 · Interactive lecture-supported reviewer

Whole-lesson orientation

Hemodynamic Morphology at a Glance

pp. 2–3
Must Know

The laboratory connects fluid imbalance and vascular flow disturbances to their gross and microscopic morphology. The central arc is: edema/congestion → thrombosis → embolization → infarction, with chronic venous congestion producing hemorrhage, hemosiderin, and heart failure cells.

Altered flow or pressureHyperemia, congestion, hydrostatic imbalance
→
Fluid or blood accumulationEdema, engorged vessels, hemorrhage
→
Thrombus formationMural thrombus, Lines of Zahn
→
Embolization and infarctionSaddle embolus, splenic white infarct
Topic anchorGross clueMicroscopic clueClinical/case link
Appendix congestionReddish, enlarged appendix with prominent serosal vessels and fibrinopurulent exudateCongested/distended vessels; neutrophils in muscularis; luminal exudateAcute appendicitis with fever and RLQ rebound tenderness
Acute pulmonary congestionDistended lungs; frothy material on pressureCongested capillaries and smooth-to-floccular pink alveolar materialSudden dyspnea after accidental IV volume overload
Chronic pulmonary congestionHeavy, dark brown, solid lung; brown indurationFibrotic septa, congested capillaries, transudate, heart failure cellsLong-standing congestion and congestive heart failure association
Splenic infarctionPale, wedge-shaped, subcapsular lesionCoagulative necrosis: preserved outlines, eosinophilia, loss of nucleiCardiac thromboemboli such as atrial fibrillation, post-MI, infective endocarditis
ThromboembolismLong tubular thrombus; saddle embolus at pulmonary trunk bifurcationLuminal occlusion, infarcted lung, possible Lines of ZahnDVT with immobility/hypercoagulability/postoperative state; fatal pulmonary obstruction

High-yield anchors

  • Transudate: specific gravity < 1.012, low protein and cell content, due to hydrostatic imbalance.
  • Heart failure cells: hemosiderin-laden alveolar macrophages associated with pulmonary congestion in congestive heart failure.
  • Lines of Zahn: alternating pale fibrin/platelet layers and darker RBC/WBC layers in antemortem thrombi.
  • White splenic infarct: pale, well-demarcated wedge with broad edge at the capsule.

Source-scope note

The lecture title and objectives mention shock, but the slide sequence itself provides dedicated morphology and exercises for edema, congestion, thrombosis, infarction, and thromboembolism. This reviewer therefore does not add unsupported shock content.

Lesson starts here

Congestion in Acute Appendicitis

pp. 4–8, 56–60
Case

An 11-year-old male had one day of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting, temperature 39°C, and RLQ rebound tenderness. Appendectomy was performed for acute appendicitis; the appendix was swollen, enlarged, and had prominent serosal blood vessels.

Gross pathologic appendix

  • Early appendicitis is indicated by hyperemia and edema.
  • The surface is reddish and enlarged with prominent blood vessels.
  • The serosa may be covered by a thick, creamy, fibrinopurulent exudate.
Clinical Cue

In this lecture case, fever plus RLQ rebound tenderness is correlated with the grossly hyperemic, edematous appendix.

Normal versus pathologic layers

LayerNormal appendixPathologic appendix
SerosaCovers the surfaceCongested vessels
MuscularisInner circular and outer longitudinal smooth muscleInvaded by neutrophils
SubmucosaContains lymphoid tissueDistended blood vessels with RBCs in their lumina
MucosaSimple columnar epithelium with intestinal glandsUnusual number of lymphoid tissues
Lumen—Filled with exudate
Inflammatory cytokine release
→
Vasodilation
→
Increased blood flow / hyperemia
→
Immune-cell transmigrationNeutrophils predominate in acute inflammation
Key Differentiator

Active hyperemia is described as bright red from engorgement with oxygenated blood. Passive hyperemia/congestion is dusky or bluish-red because of blood stasis and impaired venous outflow.

Exam Trap

For the prominent serosal vessels in the appendix case, the lecture answer is vasodilation—not necrosis or edema. For passive hyperemia, the paired answer is dusky, blood stasis.

Acute hemodynamic lung change

Acute Pulmonary Congestion and Edema

pp. 9–11, 61–65
Case

A 75-year-old man with ischemic heart disease developed sudden difficulty breathing after accidental IV volume overload.

Gross morphology

  • The lungs appear distended.
  • Normal lungs are expected to be pink and shiny.
  • When the dissected lung is pressed, frothy material is extruded.
  • The slide marks a remaining uninvolved portion at the base as non-pathologic.

Microscopic morphology

  • Normal alveolar walls are thin and delicate; alveoli are well aerated.
  • In acute congestion, alveoli contain a smooth to slightly floccular pink material, characteristic of pulmonary edema.
  • Alveolar capillaries are congested with many RBCs.
  • The lecture associates pulmonary congestion and edema with heart failure and areas of lung inflammation.
Accidental IV fluid overload
→
Increased pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure
→
Fluid leakage into alveoli
→
Protein-poor watery transudate

What is present?

Alveolar granular/pink precipitate and alveolar capillary congestion.

What is absent acutely?

Interstitial fibrosis is a chronic change and is not expected in the acute setting.

Fluid identity

Transudate, not necrotic debris, thrombus, or an inflammatory exudate.

Do Not Confuse

The lecture contrasts a transudate—protein-poor fluid produced by hydrostatic pressure—with an exudate, which is protein-rich and linked to inflammation or infection such as pneumonia.

Long-standing venous congestion

Chronic Pulmonary Congestion

pp. 12–22, 66–70

Gross pathologic lung

  • Enlarged and heavy; heavier than the average lung.
  • Dark, reddish-brown, and very solid rather than light and spongy.
  • Diffuse involvement: lobes and parenchyma share the same abnormality, without a localized mass or cavity.
  • Brown induration is the gross pathologic change caused by CPC.
  • Squeezing the parenchyma may produce frothy fluid; fluid in the parenchymal spaces is linked to breathlessness.
Mechanism Sequence

Long-standing vascular congestion → microcapillary rupture and hemorrhage → RBC hemolysis → hemosiderin release → uptake by pulmonary macrophages → heart failure cells.

Microscopic triad

  • Thickened and fibrotic alveolar walls/septa.
  • Congested alveolar capillaries.
  • Pink transudate in alveolar spaces due to increased hydrostatic pressure.
  • Increased hemosiderin-laden macrophages—the lecture’s heart failure cells.

Normal lung baseline

  • Very spongy, almost translucent at edges, pink and shiny, and aerated.
  • Alveolar walls/septa are thin and delicate; alveolar spaces contain air.
  • Alveoli contain type I and II pneumocytes; alveolar sacs allow gas exchange.
  • Alveolar ducts collect and distribute air; respiratory bronchioles have a single alveolar lining, ciliated cuboidal epithelium, and Clara cells linked to detoxification.

Transudate profile

Specific gravity< 1.012
  • Low protein content
  • Low cell content
  • Produced by hydrostatic imbalance
FeatureAcute pulmonary congestionChronic pulmonary congestion
Capillary congestionPresentPresent
Edema/transudatePresentPresent
Interstitial/alveolar septal fibrosisNot an acute featureCharacteristic chronic change
Heart failure cellsNot the defining acute featureCharacteristic chronic finding
Gross appearanceDistended, frothyHeavy, dark brown, solid; brown induration
Exam Trap

The pair that differentiates chronic from acute congestion is fibrosis and heart failure cells. Heart failure cells are macrophages, not neutrophils, lymphocytes, or eosinophils.

Arterial occlusion in a solid organ

Infarcted Spleen

pp. 23–28, 71–75

Gross splenic infarct

  • Enlarged spleen or splenomegaly.
  • Bland, pale, wedge-shaped subcapsular infarct.
  • The broad edge abuts the capsule.
  • The spleen may appear brown and firm; fibrin exudate may be seen on the capsular surface.
  • A white infarct is well demarcated, pale, and pyramidal/wedge/triangular.
Clinical Association

The lecture lists thromboemboli from the heart as causes, including atrial fibrillation, post-myocardial infarction, and infective endocarditis.

Histology

  • A clear line of demarcation separates normal and infarcted spleen.
  • The infarcted area is pink, homogeneous, and less cellular.
  • Inflammatory cells are present along the margin.
  • At higher power: preserved tissue outlines, increased eosinophilia, and loss of nuclei.

Normal spleen landmarks

  • Capsule: dense connective tissue with fibrous trabeculae.
  • White pulp: lymphatic nodules with central arteries.
  • Red pulp: venous sinuses and splenic cords of Billroth.

Why “white”?

The slide explains that the tissue density prevents blood from nearby capillaries from flowing back into the necrotic area. White infarcts are stated to be more common than red infarcts.

Necrosis typeGross architectureHistology
Coagulative necrosisIntact architecturePreserved cell outlines, eosinophilia, loss of nuclei; structures remain preserved for a few days
Liquefactive necrosisLoss of architecture; may have an abscessNeutrophils at the center, inflammation, and digestion of dead cells
High-Yield Recap

Spleen + embolus from the heart → pale/white infarct. The dominant histologic pattern of the infarct is coagulative necrosis.

Thrombus traveling to pulmonary circulation

Thromboembolism

pp. 29–30, 76–80

Gross recognition

  • A thrombus may appear as a long, tubular structure resembling the size of leg veins.
  • A saddle embolus lodges at the bifurcation of the pulmonary trunk.
  • The source is commonly a deep venous thrombosis from a lower-limb vein.
  • Complete luminal occlusion obstructs pulmonary blood flow and may lead to death.

Predisposing context in the lecture

  • Deep vein thrombosis
  • Immobility
  • Hypercoagulability
  • Postoperative state
  • Long plane trip in the classic lab case

Classic clinical presentation

The lab exercise describes a patient with DVT who develops sudden breathlessness and acute right heart failure (cor pulmonale) after a long plane trip, then collapses and dies.

Microscopic comparison

  • A normal pulmonary artery has a clear lumen; surrounding alveoli are clear.
  • Pulmonary embolism shows luminal occlusion and infarcted lung parenchyma.
  • The thromboembolus contains fibrin admixed with red blood cells and acute inflammatory cells in the annotated image.
  • Look for Lines of Zahn as evidence of an antemortem thrombus.
Exam Trap

The detailed lecture description of Lines of Zahn is pale platelet/fibrin layers alternating with darker RBC/WBC layers. In the simplified exercise choice, the answer is summarized as fibrin and red blood cells.

SGD Picture A

Generalized / Pitting Edema

pp. 31–35
“A Ticking Time Bomb”

A 65-year-old woman with diabetes, prior heart attack, sedentary lifestyle, and recently diagnosed DVT arrives with severe dyspnea. Her blood pressure is elevated and her legs are swollen. She later coughs violently and dies.

Describe the finding

  • The dorsum and ankle are unusually swollen, consistent with subcutaneous fluid accumulation.
  • The skin appears stretched and shiny.
  • Pressure leaves an indentation or “pit” with reduced elastic recoil that remains after pressure is released.
Lecture-Specific Note

The slides call the finding generalized edema and also pitting edema.

Mechanisms that produce edema

1 Impaired venous return

Deep vein thrombosis impairs venous return. The resulting increase in hydrostatic pressure drives fluid from the intravascular compartment into the interstitial space.

2 Reduced oncotic pull

Decreased plasma oncotic pressure allows fluid to accumulate in the interstitium.

3 Lymphatic obstruction

Reduced lymphatic reabsorption permits excess interstitial fluid to remain.

4 Sodium and water retention / inflammation

The lecture also lists sodium and water retention and inflammatory states as settings where edema occurs.

MechanismLecture examples
Increased hydrostatic pressureCongestive heart failure, liver cirrhosis
Reduced plasma oncotic pressureNephrotic syndrome, kwashiorkor, liver cirrhosis
Lymphatic obstructionFilariasis, radiation therapy, removal of lymph nodes
InflammationAcute inflammation, chronic inflammation, granulation tissue
Sodium and water retentionAcute renal failure, increased salt intake
SGD Picture B

Mural Thrombus

pp. 36–39

Identification and description

  • A thrombus is a pathologic form of blood clot that can cause ischemia and embolism.
  • The pictured lesion is a mural thrombus or non-occlusive thrombus.
  • It can develop over areas of myocardial infarct, myocarditis, and aneurysm.
  • It is attached to a large capacitance vessel wall, including the aorta or ventricular/atrial chambers.

How it forms: hemostasis sequence

Endothelial injuryInitiates a hemostatic plug
→
Vessel contractionResponse to injury
→
Primary hemostasisUnstable platelet aggregate
→
Secondary hemostasisCoagulation cascade forms a stable plug/thrombus
Lecture Sequence Detail

Platelets adhere and aggregate at the injury site; secondary hemostasis activates the coagulation cascade. RBCs and WBCs can become trapped, increasing clot size.

Possible fates of a thrombus

Dissolution

Anticoagulant systems and blood flow gradually break down the thrombus (thrombolysis), allowing it to shrink or disappear.

Recanalization

New channels form within the thrombus, partially reopening flow; some obstruction may remain.

Embolization

A portion detaches, travels as an embolus, and may block a smaller vessel.

Propagation / organization

The exercise lists propagation and organization with recanalization among recognized outcomes.

SGD Picture C

Pulmonary Thromboembolism

pp. 40–42

Gross description

  • The arrow identifies a pulmonary embolus / saddle embolus originating from a DVT.
  • The lungs appear relatively reddish compared with normal pink lungs.
  • A dark red clot obstructs the pulmonary arteries.
Large lower-extremity veinsPopliteal, femoral, or iliac thrombus
→
Embolus dislodges
→
IVC
→
Right atrium
→
Right ventricle
→
Pulmonary artery bifurcationMain left and right pulmonary arteries
Clinical Cue

DVT + sudden severe dyspnea + collapse is the lecture’s recurring pulmonary thromboembolism pattern.

If You See X, Think Y

If a long tubular clot is lodged across the pulmonary trunk bifurcation, think saddle pulmonary embolus from lower-limb DVT.

SGD Picture D

Antemortem versus Postmortem Clot

pp. 43–50
Lines of Zahn

A characteristic feature of antemortem thrombi: alternating light pink layers of fibrin and platelets with dark pink layers of RBCs and WBCs.

FeatureAntemortem clotPostmortem clot
Gross appearanceDry, rough, granular; may or may not fit vascular contoursMoist, jelly-like, smooth; forms a perfect cast of the vessel wall
AttachmentAttached to the endotheliumNo attachment
EndotheliumDamaged or injuredNo endothelial damage
CompositionPlatelets, clotting factors, RBCs, WBCsMostly fibrin with RBCs and WBCs
Lines of ZahnPresent; laminatedAbsent; non-laminated
ConditionFormed intravascularly in a living personFormed after death; the slide also lists post-phlebotomy procedures

Postmortem layering

  • Chicken-fat clot: yellow and fatty-looking; represents the upper portion after settling by gravity.
  • Currant-jelly clot: dark red and gelatinous due to the preponderance of RBCs settling to the bottom when circulation stagnates after death.
Fastest Distinction

Attached + rough/granular + laminated Lines of Zahn = antemortem thrombus. Unattached + smooth/jelly-like + vessel cast = postmortem clot.

SGD Picture E

Chronic Passive Congestion of the Lungs

pp. 51–54

Identify and describe

  • Chronic passive congestion (CPC) of the lung.
  • Thickened alveolar septa containing congested capillaries.
  • Pulmonary edema.
  • Hemosiderin-laden macrophages, also called heart failure cells because of their association with pulmonary congestion in congestive heart failure.

Fluid in the alveolar space

Transudate: protein-poor with little to no cellular content. The edema is described as non-inflammatory and linked to increased hydrostatic pressure in lung alveoli and pulmonary vessels.

Lecture pathophysiology sequence

Heart failure → hemostasis → excess blood enters the extravascular space → RBCs are phagocytosed by macrophages → hemosiderin is released as brown granules.

The wording “hemostasis” is retained from the lecture slide.

Pattern Recognition

Thick fibrotic septa + congested capillaries + transudate + brown macrophages is the integrated CPC pattern.

Pages 55–80 distilled

Lab Exercise Rationales

pp. 55–80

The lecture’s multiple-choice exercises are converted here into answer-and-rationale takeaways rather than a quiz.

Appendix

Vasodilation and hyperemia

Prominent serosal vessels reflect vasodilation. Cytokine-mediated inflammation increases blood flow and supports immune-cell transmigration; neutrophils predominate in acute inflammation.

Appendix

Passive hyperemia

The organ becomes dusky because of blood stasis. A bright red organ engorged with oxygenated blood describes active hyperemia instead.

Acute pulmonary congestion

Acute histologic set

Alveolar granular precipitate and capillary congestion are present. Interstitial fibrosis is not an acute finding.

Acute pulmonary congestion

Alveolar fluid

The fluid is transudate because IV overload raises hydrostatic pressure and causes leakage of protein-poor watery fluid into alveoli.

Chronic pulmonary congestion

Chronic differentiators

Fibrosis and heart failure cells distinguish chronic from acute congestion; capillary congestion and edema can occur in both.

Chronic pulmonary congestion

Cell identity

Heart failure cells are macrophages that have ingested hemosiderin from degraded RBCs.

Splenic infarct

White infarct setting

Spleen + embolus from the heart produces a pale, well-demarcated, often triangular infarct in a solid organ.

Splenic infarct

Dominant necrosis

An infarct is a localized area of coagulative necrosis.

Thromboembolism

Lines of Zahn

Pale platelet/fibrin laminations alternate with darker RBC/WBC layers. The simplified answer choice is fibrin and RBCs.

Thrombus outcomes

Recognized fates

Propagation, lysis/resolution, embolization, and organization/recanalization. The exercise identifies infection as the exception.

High-yield distinctions

Comparisons

lecture-wide
PairFirst conceptSecond concept
Active hyperemia vs passive congestionBright red; engorged with oxygenated blood; vasodilation/increased flowDusky/bluish-red; impaired venous outflow and blood stasis
Acute vs chronic pulmonary congestionCapillary congestion + alveolar edema; no defining interstitial fibrosisCongestion + transudate + fibrosis + heart failure cells; brown induration
Transudate vs exudateProtein-poor, low-cell fluid from hydrostatic imbalance; CPC SG 1.012Protein-rich fluid associated with inflammation or infection
Coagulative vs liquefactive necrosisArchitecture intact; preserved outlines, eosinophilia, loss of nucleiArchitecture lost; abscess may be present; central neutrophils and digestion
Thrombus vs embolusPathologic clot formed at a site and potentially attached to a wallDetached portion traveling in blood and capable of occluding a distant vessel
Antemortem vs postmortem clotAttached, dry/rough/granular, laminated Lines of ZahnUnattached, moist/smooth/jelly-like, non-laminated vessel cast
White infarct vs congested/hemorrhagic tissuePale, wedge-shaped, well demarcated; solid organ arterial occlusionDark/dusky or reddish tissue from congestion, blood stasis, or hemorrhage
Do Not Confuse

Heart failure cells are macrophages containing hemosiderin—not cardiac muscle cells.

Do Not Confuse

Frothy lung fluid in the volume-overload/CPC context is tied to pulmonary edema and transudate, not a thrombus in the alveolar space.

Do Not Confuse

Lines of Zahn indicate antemortem thrombus; chicken-fat and currant-jelly layering are postmortem clot patterns.

Final rapid review

Cram Sheet

pp. 1–80

Appendix congestion

  • Gross: red, enlarged, prominent vessels; thick creamy fibrinopurulent serosal exudate.
  • Micro: congested serosa, neutrophils in muscularis, distended submucosal vessels, luminal exudate.
  • Exercise: serosal vessels = vasodilation.

Acute pulmonary congestion

  • Gross: distended lungs, frothy material.
  • Micro: congested capillaries + smooth/floccular pink alveolar material.
  • Mechanism: hydrostatic pressure ↑ → alveolar leakage → transudate.
  • Not acute: interstitial fibrosis.

Chronic pulmonary congestion

  • Gross: heavy, dark brown, solid; brown induration.
  • Micro: thick fibrotic septa, congested capillaries, transudate, heart failure cells.
  • Sequence: congestion → microcapillary rupture → RBC breakdown → hemosiderin → macrophages.
  • Transudate SG: < 1.012.

Heart failure cells

  • Identity: hemosiderin-laden alveolar macrophages.
  • Appearance: brown granules in macrophage cytoplasm.
  • Association: chronic pulmonary congestion with congestive heart failure.

Splenic infarct

  • Gross: pale, wedge-shaped, subcapsular, broad edge at capsule.
  • Cause: cardiac thromboemboli—AF, post-MI, infective endocarditis.
  • Micro: line of demarcation, pink homogeneous less-cellular area, marginal inflammation.
  • Necrosis: coagulative—preserved outlines, eosinophilia, loss of nuclei.

Saddle pulmonary embolus

  • Source: lower-extremity DVT.
  • Route: popliteal/femoral/iliac → IVC → RA → RV → pulmonary bifurcation.
  • Outcome: pulmonary flow obstruction, acute right heart failure, collapse/death.

Mural thrombus

  • Location: aorta or ventricular/atrial chamber wall.
  • Setting: MI, myocarditis, aneurysm.
  • Fates: dissolution, propagation, embolization, organization/recanalization.

Lines of Zahn

  • Pale layers: fibrin + platelets.
  • Dark layers: RBCs + WBCs.
  • Meaning: laminated antemortem thrombus.

Postmortem clot

  • Gross: moist, smooth, jelly-like, vessel cast, unattached.
  • Chicken fat: yellow upper layer.
  • Currant jelly: dark red lower layer rich in settled RBCs.

Edema mechanisms

  • Hydrostatic pressure ↑
  • Plasma oncotic pressure ↓
  • Lymphatic obstruction
  • Inflammation
  • Sodium/water retention
Last-Minute Pattern Grid

Dusky + stasis → passive congestion.   Frothy lung + pink alveolar fluid → pulmonary edema/transudate.   Brown macrophages + fibrosis → chronic pulmonary congestion.   Pale wedge in spleen → white infarct/coagulative necrosis.   Attached laminated clot → antemortem thrombus.   DVT → pulmonary bifurcation → saddle embolus.
